notable.notable_blurb: Mayor of New Haven and Cleveland; Yale donor

narrative_blocks.header: His bank failure cost Yale its endowment
narrative_blocks.body: As president of New Haven’s Eagle Bank he made badly undercollateralized loans, and its 1825 collapse cut Yale’s endowment to $1,800. He left for Cleveland in disgrace in 1830 — and was later its eighth mayor.

narrative_blocks.header: One gift tied two branches of the tree
narrative_blocks.body: In 1827 he gave Yale a Morse portrait of Eli Whitney, accession 1827.1 — one of the gallery’s earliest catalogued works. Whitney had married a Hooker descendant, as had Hoadley himself, so the single gift quietly joined two branches of this family.

orbit_connections.name: George Hoadley (Mayor of New Haven and Cleveland)
orbit_connections.relationship: Second husband of Mary Ann Woolsey Hoadley (HD2075)
orbit_connections.notes: George Hoadley (Dec 15, 1781 - Feb 20, 1857). 4th Mayor of New Haven; 8th Mayor of Cleveland. As Eagle Bank president in 1825 made reckless loans that collapsed the bank, reducing Yale endowment to $1,800. Moved to Cleveland in disgrace 1830. Their son George Hoadly (dropping the e) became 36th Governor of Ohio. (Wikipedia)

orbit_connections.name: George Hoadly (36th Governor of Ohio)
orbit_connections.relationship: Son of Mary Ann Woolsey Hoadley (HD2075) and George Hoadley
orbit_connections.notes: George Hoadly (Jul 31, 1826 - Aug 26, 1902). 36th Governor of Ohio 1884-1886. Studied law with Salmon P. Chase. Superior Court judge. Governor during the Cincinnati Riots of 1884 (45-50 dead). The Hooker line through the Puritan chain governing Ohio in the Gilded Age: Thomas Hooker - Mary Hooker Pierpont - Jonathan Edwards - Mary Edwards Dwight - Elizabeth Dwight Woolsey - Mary Ann Woolsey - George Hoadly (governor). (Wikipedia)
